Title: Arabidopsis guard cell chloroplasts import cytosolic ATP for starch turnover and stomatal opening

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes guard cell chloroplast starch accumulation/turnover and proper stomatal opening by importing cytosolic ATP into guard cell chloroplasts; ntt1 guard cells are nearly starchless and show impaired stomatal opening
Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Plastid inner-envelope ATP/ADP antiporter; NUCLEOTIDE TRANSPORTER family
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Highly and preferentially expressed in guard cells of mature leaves; transcript level ~36-fold higher in guard cell-enriched epidermal peels than whole leaves; promoter-GUS strong in guard cells
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RT-qPCR; promoter-GUS; isolated chloroplast ATP uptake assay with stromal AT1.03 sensor; T-DNA mutant phenotyping; guard cell starch imaging; whole-plant gas exchange/stomatal conductance
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NTT1 is the major plastidial ATP/ADP translocator expressed in Arabidopsis guard cells. 2. Guard cell chloroplasts import exogenous ATP, unlike mature mesophyll chloroplasts, consistent with NTT1 activity. 3. ntt1 mutants are nearly devoid of guard cell starch and have strongly impaired stomatal opening kinetics, indicating imported cytosolic ATP is required for guard cell starch turnover and stomatal function.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target to improve stomatal dynamics and guard-cell carbon/energy management, but manipulation may affect gas exchange and water-use traits; guard-cell-specific deployment would be preferable.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Dark-to-light transition; white light; end of night, 2 h and 8 h into day
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Classified as Common Gene because it is a transporter, not an enzyme or transcriptional regulator. The paper identifies NTT1 as the major isoform in guard cells relative to NTT2.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: ntt1 shows impaired stomatal opening and closing kinetics and slight reduction in photosynthetic assimilation.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contributes to guard cell chloroplast ATP import and starch accumulation, but has a weaker effect than NTT1; ntt2 shows reduced guard cell starch yet near-WT stomatal opening
Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Plastid inner-envelope ATP/ADP antiporter; NUCLEOTIDE TRANSPORTER family
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Preferentially expressed in guard cells of mature leaves but weaker than NTT1; transcript level ~4-fold higher in guard cell-enriched epidermal peels than whole leaves; weaker promoter-GUS in guard cells
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RT-qPCR; promoter-GUS; T-DNA mutant phenotyping; guard cell starch imaging; whole-plant gas exchange/stomatal conductance
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NTT2 is guard-cell expressed but less abundant than NTT1. 2. ntt2 mutants have reduced guard cell starch, confirming a contributory role in ATP import-linked starch metabolism. 3. Stomatal conductance kinetics in ntt2 are comparable to wild type, indicating NTT2 is a minor isoform for this trait.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Secondary target for tuning guard-cell ATP import; likely less influential alone than NTT1.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Dark-to-light transition; white light; end of night, 2 h and 8 h into day
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Classified as Common Gene because it is a transporter. The paper concludes NTT1 is the predominant guard-cell isoform.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced guard cell starch but limited effect on stomatal opening under tested conditions.

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Guard cell sugar import supporting starch biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Mesophyll-derived hexose uptake into guard cells
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: imported cytosolic/apoplastic hexose pool in guard cells
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Mediates proton-coupled monosaccharide transport across the guard cell plasma membrane, importing mesophyll-derived hexoses used for guard cell starch biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Monosaccharide H+ symporter; Sugar Transport Protein family
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for efficient import of mesophyll-derived glucose into guard cells and thus for normal guard cell starch accumulation at dawn; stp1stp4 mutants are nearly starchless and show reduced starch accumulation in isolated guard cells
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Use of stp1stp4 double mutant in isolated guard cell starch accumulation assays under red light ± DCMU; prior mutant evidence cited within Results
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. STP1 functions with STP4 in guard-cell monosaccharide uptake. 2. Loss of STP1/STP4 greatly reduces guard-cell starch accumulation, demonstrating that imported hexoses are major carbon precursors for guard-cell starch synthesis. 3. In DCMU-treated isolated guard cells, the stp1stp4 phenotype helped separate apoplastic sugar import from photosynthesis-derived carbon contribution.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential leverage point to tune guard-cell sugar uptake and stomatal behavior, though likely coupled to broader carbon and water-use effects.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: End of night; red light illumination; isolated epidermal peels; DCMU treatment
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included as a core experimental gene because stp1stp4 mutant was directly used in Results to dissect carbon source for guard-cell starch synthesis. Although STP1 is a transporter rather than enzyme, it is placed under Pathway_Genes here due to its direct precursor-supply role in the metabolic process.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss reduces guard-cell glucose and starch, likely compromising stomatal function.

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Guard cell sugar import supporting starch biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Mesophyll-derived hexose uptake into guard cells
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: imported cytosolic/apoplastic hexose pool in guard cells
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Mediates proton-coupled monosaccharide transport across the guard cell plasma membrane, importing mesophyll-derived hexoses used for guard cell starch biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Monosaccharide H+ symporter; Sugar Transport Protein family
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Acts with STP1 to support mesophyll-derived glucose uptake into guard cells and normal guard cell starch accumulation; loss in stp1stp4 lowers starch accumulation especially in isolated guard cells
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Use of stp1stp4 double mutant in isolated guard cell starch accumulation assays under red light ± DCMU; prior mutant evidence cited within Results
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. STP4 partners with STP1 in guard-cell monosaccharide uptake. 2. Combined loss of STP1/STP4 greatly limits starch accumulation in guard cells, supporting imported hexose as a major carbon source. 3. DCMU responses in stp1stp4 guard cells revealed a residual contribution of guard-cell photosynthesis to starch synthesis when plasma-membrane sugar import is defective.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate for modifying guard-cell carbohydrate status and stomatal kinetics, but with possible pleiotropic impacts on leaf carbon-water balance.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: End of night; red light illumination; isolated epidermal peels; DCMU treatment
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included as a core experimental gene because stp1stp4 mutant was directly used in Results. Functionally a transporter rather than a catalytic enzyme.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss reduces starch accumulation and may impair stomatal performance.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts glucose-1-phosphate and ATP to ADP-glucose, the activated glucosyl donor for starch biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Abundant in guard cell protoplasts, supporting active starch biosynthetic capacity in guard cell chloroplasts
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Immunoblot analysis comparing GCPs and MCPs; protein titration versus RubisCO
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AGPase protein is readily detectable and relatively abundant in guard cell protoplasts. 2. Guard cells contain more AGPase than RubisCO by immunoblot titration, consistent with prioritization of starch synthesis capacity over photosynthetic carbon fixation capacity. 3. These data support the model that imported ATP fuels starch biosynthesis in guard cell chloroplasts.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: May be useful in engineering guard-cell starch metabolism if combined with ATP import and sugar import traits.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Comparative protein profiling in guard cell and mesophyll protoplasts
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Measured as protein abundance support rather than via direct mutant perturbation in this paper.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Critical enzyme of starch biosynthesis; presented as abundant in guard cells but rate-limiting status not directly established.

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Anaplerotic CO2 fixation / malate metabolism in guard cells
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Fixes CO2/HCO3- onto phosphoenolpyruvate to form oxaloacetate, feeding malate production in guard cells
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Highly abundant in guard cells relative to mesophyll cells, supporting the conclusion that anaplerotic CO2 fixation via PEPc is the main carbon assimilation route in Arabidopsis guard cells
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Immunoblot comparison of GCPs and MCPs
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PEPc protein is present at high abundance in guard cell protoplasts, higher than in mesophyll cells. 2. This supports a dominant role for anaplerotic CO2 fixation in guard-cell metabolism. 3. The resulting carbon flux likely feeds malate formation and energy/osmotic functions associated with stomatal opening.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target for modifying guard-cell malate metabolism and stomatal energetics, but effects may be highly context dependent.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Comparative protein profiling in guard cell and mesophyll protoplasts
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper emphasizes PEPc as a major route for carbon assimilation in guard cells rather than RubisCO-driven Calvin cycle flux.

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Malate metabolism / anaplerotic CO2 fixation support in guard cells
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces oxaloacetate to malate using NADH in guard-cell-associated malate metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Detected at relatively high abundance in guard cells, consistent with active conversion of OAA to malate and a central role of malate in guard-cell metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Immunoblot comparison of GCPs and MCPs
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MDH4 protein is present in guard cells and more abundant than in mesophyll cells. 2. This supports active malate production downstream of anaplerotic CO2 fixation. 3. Malate is inferred to contribute to both osmotic function and mitochondrial energy metabolism in guard cells.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Possible target for adjusting guard-cell malate flux, though direct engineering value is not tested here.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Comparative protein profiling in guard cell and mesophyll protoplasts
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Antibody specificity note provided in Methods indicates recognition of cytosolic NAD-MDH-related protein; functional inference is based on protein abundance rather than direct perturbation.
